skill.name: changelog-automation
skill.description: Automate changelog generation from commits, PRs, and releases following Keep a Changelog format. Use when setting up release workflows, generating release notes, or standardizing commit conventions.
skill.summary: Generate automated changelogs and release notes using Conventional Commits and industry-standard versioning.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 69 patterns (external_commands: 45, network: 13, env_access: 3, blocker: 8) but all are false positives. The skill consists entirely of markdown documentation with code examples. External command patterns are bash examples in documentation blocks, URLs are reference links, and token references are GitHub Actions workflow templates for user configuration. No executable code or actual security risks detected.

content.value_statement: Manual changelog creation is error-prone and time-consuming. This skill automates release notes using Conventional Commits and Keep a Changelog format.
actual_capabilities: Generate changelogs from git commit history using Conventional Commits format
Configure automated release workflows with semantic-release or standard-version
Create GitHub Actions workflows for CI/CD release automation
Implement commit message validation with commitlint and husky
Generate release notes with git-cliff or commitizen tools
Set up semantic versioning based on commit types
limitations: Requires consistent Conventional Commits format in git history for best results
Cannot generate changelogs for projects without commit history or unreliable logs
Tool configuration requires Node.js, Rust, or Python depending on chosen method
Does not execute commands directly - provides configuration patterns only

use_cases.title: Open Source Library Maintainer
use_cases.description: Set up automated changelog generation for npm package releases using semantic-release with GitHub Actions.
use_cases.target_user: Library maintainers publishing to npm or PyPI

use_cases.title: Enterprise Release Manager
use_cases.description: Standardize commit conventions across teams and generate internal release notes with security audit sections.

use_cases.title: Solo Developer
use_cases.description: Implement lightweight changelog automation using git-cliff with minimal configuration overhead.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Changelog Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up automated changelog generation for my project. I want to use Conventional Commits and generate a CHANGELOG.md file following Keep a Changelog format.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setting up first changelog automation

prompt_templates.title: Commit Linting Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure commitlint with husky to enforce Conventional Commits in my repository. Include rules for type validation and subject line formatting.

prompt_templates.title: GitHub Actions Release Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs semantic-release on push to main, generates changelog, creates GitHub releases, and publishes to npm.
prompt_templates.scenario: Full CI/CD automation for package publishing

prompt_templates.title: Custom Changelog Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure git-cliff with a custom template that includes sections for features, bug fixes, security updates, and contributor acknowledgments with GitHub links.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced customization with specific formatting needs

output_examples.input: Generate changelog for v2.1.0 release with 15 commits including 3 features, 5 bug fixes, and 2 documentation updates
output_examples.output: CHANGELOG.md with formatted sections: Features (OAuth2 support, Dark mode, API caching), Bug Fixes (checkout race condition, login timeout, search filters, export functionality, notification delivery), Documentation (API reference updates, Migration guide)

output_examples.input: Validate commit message: 'fix: resolve memory leak in image processor'
output_examples.output: Valid Conventional Commit - type: fix, no scope, clear subject describing the change

best_practices: Use Conventional Commits format consistently to enable automation and clear history
Mark breaking changes with exclamation mark (feat!:) or BREAKING CHANGE footer for visibility
Reference issue numbers in commit footers to link changes to tracked work
anti_patterns: Manually editing generated CHANGELOG.md files - always regenerate from commit history
Mixing unrelated changes in single commits - keep one logical change per commit
Skipping commit validation in CI - always enforce conventions before merge

faq.question: What is Conventional Commits format?
faq.answer: Conventional Commits is a specification for commit messages with structure: type(scope): description. Types include feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, chore, and more.

faq.question: How do I handle breaking changes?
faq.answer: Add exclamation mark after type (feat!: description) or include BREAKING CHANGE: in commit footer with migration instructions.

faq.question: Can I use this with existing git history?
faq.answer: Yes, but results depend on commit quality. For inconsistent history, consider starting changelog from a specific tag using git-cliff range options.

faq.question: Which tool should I choose?
faq.answer: Use standard-version for simple npm projects, semantic-release for full automation, git-cliff for speed and customization, or commitizen for Python projects.

faq.question: How do I exclude certain commits from changelog?
faq.answer: Configure commit parsers to skip specific types. Most tools exclude docs, style, test, and chore by default. Add skip rules for other types as needed.

faq.question: Can I publish to multiple registries?
faq.answer: Yes, semantic-release supports multiple plugins. Configure @semantic-release/npm for npm, @semantic-release/github for releases, and custom plugins for other targets.
